Episode 568: Networking Up, Part 2

July 25, 2018 by Ivan Misner 12 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

In Episode 530, Dr. Misner talked to Mike Macedonio about “networking up.” That episode focused on hanging out where successful people are and how to connect with them. Today, Dr. Misner wants to share some additional suggestions about how to network above your weight class.

  1. Embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, you’re not aiming high enough.
  2. Don’t sell to them. Nothing will shut down your chance for a real connection faster.
  3. Don’t complain to them. You don’t want to be remembered for complaining.
  4. Don’t be a sycophant. It’s fine to acknowledge their work, but don’t overdo the fandom.
  5. Work within the context of the meeting. Connect what’s happening at the moment
  6. Find out what they’re currently interested in. Do your homework before meeting them–or at least look them up on your phone at the event.
  7. Add value. Offer help with something they care about.
  8. Don’t assume they remember your name. They meet hundreds of people at every event. Remind them of your name and how they met you.
  9. Don’t sell to them! It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up, and it’s worth repeating.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 568

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Montreal, Canada this week attending a TLC event and also speaking at a BNI event.

Priscilla:
Great! That is a beautiful part of the world right there.

Ivan:
It really is. It’s absolutely beautiful! I’ve been here several times. I love it.

Today I’m talking about Networking Up part 2. So, we did a podcast- it’s podcast number 530, where I talked with Mike Macedonio about Networking Up. We’ve all heard the advice “you become the people who you hang out with,” which means that you not only need to surround yourself with successful people or however you define success, but you also need to continually network up and raise the bar for yourself over time. Now, Mike and I mostly talked about hanging out where successful people are and learning how to connect with those people. But what I want to do today is give you a number of points, about half a dozen or more, in addition to learning how to hang out where successful people are. I want to give you some other points that I think can really make a difference. You know, I’ve always believed that there’s generally plenty of room at the top, but it’s the bottom that’s really crowded. So, here are some suggestions on how you can network above your weight class and move up into networking with stronger people.

In addition to hanging out where successful people are, Podcast 530, the next thing is to embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, then you’re not aiming high enough. I’ve been there and I understand this feeling. However, you need to get past that and go talk to them. So if you meet somebody or see someone at an event, it’s really important to go on up and talk to them. Your discomfort may be a sign that this is the exact person you really should be talking to.

Here’s another one: Don’t sell to them. I can’t tell you how many times I’ve met someone for the first time and they start selling to me. I’ve seen the same thing happen when I’ve been with other business people who are far more successful than me. Don’t do it! The old adage, “It never hurts to ask, right?” It’s completely wrong! When you’re networking up, especially if you’re networking up with someone for the first time, a lot of people do that. Don’t be one of the crowd. Don’t sell to them. Don’t say, “This is my one opportunity!” Because it’ll be your last opportunity with that person if you try to sell to them.

Here’s one that surprises people that other people even do this: Don’t complain to them. If you’re networking up with somebody more successful, don’t complain to them. I know that sounds obvious, but I have been both the victim of it and I’ve seen it done to other people. I was with an incredibly successful businessman some time ago, he was meeting people in a crowd and someone he just met went on a rant about some problem with the man’s company. This guy absolutely stood out and was quietly escorted out. You do not want to be remembered for that. If you’re talking to somebody really successful, don’t lead with a complaint. If it’s more important to complain than it is to make a good connection, then do whatever you want. But if your goal is to really network and build relationships with people, don’t do that.

Here’s another one: Acknowledge their work, but don’t be a sycophant. Plenty of people will flatter them, but don’t puppy-dog-lick somebody to death. When you meet somebody that you’re just so excited to see them, don’t puppy-dog-lick them to death. Successful people, you know they are still people, they appreciate knowing that their work makes a difference. Just don’t overdo that. Be cognizant.

A really important recommendation: Work within the context of the meeting. If at all possible, find a way to connect what’s happening at the moment, something interesting in your discussion or setting. For example, I met a well-known international thought leader for the first time at the book signing for speakers at a conference. The problem was the audience was still at dinner and nobody was at the signing. So, I shared a story with him about me at a book signing that I did where the ONLY person who showed up was my mother. Priscilla, you may have heard me tell this story before. She acted like she was a fan and made such a big deal of the signing that people started crowding around the table where I was signing. I had this guy laughing so hard that it helped him remember who I was and what I did, and then he invited me to join an organization of his where I had the chance to network up with many, many people. As a matter of fact, I’m doing that here in Montreal right now with those people.

Here’s another point: Find out what they are currently interested in. This is a critical item. If you know that they are going to an event, do some internet research. If you meet them at an event or you see them at an event, get on your mobile device and just look them up. See what they are working on so that you can have a conversation on that topic- which leads me to the next suggestion:

Add value. If you don’t sell to them, what can you do? Add value to them. Here’s a great real-life example, one of the times that I met Richard Branson on Necker Island, I really wanted to do a video with him- that I put up on IvanMisner.com. I wanted to put it up there. I wanted to say, “Hey, Richard, will you do a video with me?” Instead, I asked about something he was working on, which was The B Team- which was about how business can make a difference in local communities. And he just lit up, he was very excited! I said, “How can I help you with that? What can I do to help you with that?” He said, “Well, you’re in a network, can you get the word out?” I said, “Yeah! Would you like to do a video on it?” And he said yes. So there it was! I’m adding value to him, rather than asking for a favor. And by adding value, it makes that person really want to do that, and I was able to do that. You can go to IvanMisner.com and do a search on Richard Branson and you’ll see the video that I did with him on Necker Island.

Here’s the last thing and then I want to repeat one other: If you meet them a second time, you’re networking up and you’ve already met them once, don’t assume they remember your name. That’s so important. If you meet them and connect with them again, never ever assume they remember you. Always help them out by giving them context of how you know each other or met. Really successful people tend to meet hundreds of thousands and thousands of people, so giving them context can help them jog their memory. So if you meet them in person, give them a reminder of where they met. For example; when I’m networking on an email communication and I’m networking up, I’ll often times send a copy of a photo that may have been taken of the two of us at the event and say, “Hey, we met at this event. Not sure if that jogs your memory but here’s where we met and I just wanted to reconnect.” And that helps people. Don’t ever assume they remember you.

And the last thing I want to share: Don’t sell to them! I call it “sales Tourettes.” People can’t help themselves, they just burst out trying to sell. It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up.

So there are about eight different techniques in addition to what we talked about in the last Networking Up podcast. Feel free to share this in your chapters. And if you have something that you want to add through my networking up scenario, please add it here on BNIPodcast.com. I read all of the posts that go up. That’s it for me, Priscilla, unless you have a question or want to add anything.

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s fascinating. I like all of your points. Especially the uncomfortable barometer as evidence that you’re doing the very thing that you’ve promoted here.

Ivan:
Yeah, a lot of people may think, “Oh, that’s Ivan. He can just introduce himself to anyone.” Well, you know what, I suffer from the same issue. I’m networking up and I’m uncomfortable. You just have to work past that. You’ll never have a chance to meet them if you don’t step up and introduce yourself.

Priscilla:
Right! And I love that you suggest reminding them of your name because it’s just really difficult to keep those names at the top of your head- especially if you haven’t seen that person in a while or maybe you’ve only met them once, it’s very hard to remember their name.

Ivan:
Exactly. I had somebody get really mad at me a couple of years ago because I didn’t remember his name. And here’s the kicker, he said, “You don’t remember me?” And at that moment, I was at a PTA meeting. I’m thinking, “I’ve never met this guy at a PTA before. Is he in BNI?” So I ask him if we met at a BNI event and he said, “No. I went to school with you 20 years ago at Gladstone High School.” I’m like seriously? I’m at a PTA meeting, I run a large organization, and this is just completely out of context. And had he said to me, “Hey, we went to school together at Gladstone-” the brain matter would have gone to the right file and opened that. So, there’s just this assumption that people will remember them, even in a different context. Don’t make that assumption.

Priscilla:
Okay, cool. Well, thank you, Dr. Misner! I think that’s great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Yvonne Katamba says

    July 25, 2018 at 3:21 am

    I like all the comments if Networking up but I have liked and been reminded Do not sell to them!
    I have made mistakes in the past.

    Reply

  2. HUMBERTO SANCHEZ GARCIA says

    July 26, 2018 at 9:17 pm

    Necesitamos los podcast en español.

    Reply

    • Sallie Goetsch says

      July 26, 2018 at 10:24 pm

      You can translate the transcript using the Google translate button–imperfect, but better than nothing. It may be that one of the Spanish-speaking BNI regions needs to create its own podcast. Talk to your regional director.

      Reply

  3. John Demato says

    July 29, 2018 at 10:02 am

    You brought the Horse to water… Now how do you make them drink?

    When , how do you start the exchange to do business?

    Great information, but is missing next step…

    Reply

  4. Ivan Misner says

    August 1, 2018 at 3:28 pm

    John, the “one secret to success” at networking is that there is no one secret. The answer to your question exists in the breadth and depth of the 569 podcasts that exist on this site. Listen to about 400 of them and I think you’ll get what I’m talking about.

    Reply

  5. Hugh Grey says

    August 7, 2018 at 2:12 pm

    One quote I heard around this is to be a friend not a fan. Help them like a friend instead of trying to get their autograph.

    Reply

  6. Mack McCoy says

    August 22, 2018 at 2:36 am

    Couldn’t these be the 9 Commandments of Networking?

    Reply

  7. Oudi says

    September 4, 2018 at 4:23 am

    Hi Dr Ivan you asked for any additional tips. I would suggest not talking the person’s ear off. Ask questions and keep your turn to talk short n punchy. Having had the pleasure of entertaining Graham your CEO you can let me know if I practised what I have just preached to you. Graham is fantastic and so was this podcast. Bijay Shaw is a great ND here in the UAE by the way. Grateful to be part of the organization and pleasure serving as the Exec Director for Abu Dhabi.

    Reply

  8. Martin Culbert says

    March 6, 2019 at 4:40 pm

    Guilty as charged. I will carry this information and set reminders to change this behavior. Thank you Dr. Misner!

    Reply

    • Ivan Misner says

      March 6, 2019 at 5:12 pm

      Don’t feel too guilty, Martin. How do you think I learned… I was “guilty as charged” too.

      Ivan

      Reply

  9. Wes Geib says

    July 6, 2022 at 6:08 am

    Hello Ivan! I listened to this podcast yesterday, and it reminded me of the only time I was ever in your presence. It was a few years ago, and we were both attending the funeral of the late great “Chick” Gallagher. You were in the viewing line, just a person ahead of me. I wanted to introduce myself, but I didn’t think it was an appropriate time and place, and I didn’t get a chance later.
    I first want to say how impressed I was that you and some other BNI leadership team were in attendance, that says a lot about both “Chick” and you guys. Secondly, I was curious about your thoughts on that situation in regards to networking.
    Thank you for all that you do for us!
    Wes Geib
    BNI Hershey chapter

    Reply

    • Ivan Misner says

      July 6, 2022 at 2:21 pm

      Hi Wes, thanks for your post.

      I think it is ok to introduce yourself in a situation like you describe. The key in a situation like this is to always “honor the event.” If someone were to launch into their elevator pitch – that would be totally inappropriate. However, to introduce yourself and talk about your experience with the person who has passed away is completely in context and appropriate.

      I hope our paths cross again. Hopefully in a better situation.

      Ivan

      Reply
